content.text: Image captioning is the task of generating a natural language description for an image. Unlike image classification, which maps an image to a fixed label, captioning produces free-form text — meaning the model must both understand the visual content and decode it into grammatically coherent sentences.

A good caption must identify objects and their attributes, describe spatial relationships, capture actions and interactions, and maintain linguistic fluency — all simultaneously. This demands tight coupling between a visual processing module and a language generation module, and the history of image captioning is largely the story of how that coupling has evolved.
Three generations of architecture define the field: CNN+RNN pipelines (2014–2016), attention-enhanced RNNs (2015–2019), and transformer-based encoder-decoder models (2019–present).

attrs.alt: Image captioning architecture evolution: CNN+RNN (Show and Tell), attention-based captioning (Show Attend and Tell), and transformer models (BLIP-2, CoCa), with a comparison table of CIDEr scores

content.text: Show and Tell (Vinyals et al., 2014) established the canonical baseline. A CNN — specifically InceptionV3 — maps the input image to a single 2048-dimensional vector. That vector initialises an LSTM, which then autoregressively generates one word at a time until an end-of-sequence token is emitted.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torchvision.models as models

class ShowAndTell(nn.Module):
    """Simplified Show and Tell architecture."""
    def __init__(self, embed_dim=256, hidden_dim=512, vocab_size=10000):
        super().__init__()
        # Visual encoder: InceptionV3 without final classification layer
        inception = models.inception_v3(pretrained=True, aux_logits=False)
        self.encoder = nn.Sequential(*list(inception.children())[:-1])
        self.pool = nn.AdaptiveAvgPool2d((1, 1))
        self.visual_proj = nn.Linear(2048, embed_dim)

        # Language decoder: LSTM
        self.word_embed = nn.Embedding(vocab_size, embed_dim)
        self.lstm = nn.LSTMCell(embed_dim, hidden_dim)
        self.output = nn.Linear(hidden_dim, vocab_size)

    def forward(self, images, captions):
        # Encode image → single vector
        with torch.no_grad():
            feats = self.encoder(images)          # (B, 2048, 1, 1)
        feats = self.pool(feats).flatten(1)       # (B, 2048)
        visual = self.visual_proj(feats)          # (B, embed_dim)

        # Initialize LSTM with visual features
        h = torch.zeros(images.size(0), 512, device=images.device)
        c = torch.zeros_like(h)
        h, c = self.lstm(visual, (h, c))          # visual token at t=0

        # Decode words
        logits = []
        for t in range(captions.size(1) - 1):
            word_embed = self.word_embed(captions[:, t])
            h, c = self.lstm(word_embed, (h, c))
            logits.append(self.output(h))

        return torch.stack(logits, dim=1)         # (B, T-1, vocab_size)

The simplicity is also the weakness: the entire image is compressed into one vector before any language generation begins, so the model cannot selectively attend to the region of the image most relevant to the word it is currently generating. A word like "ball" should focus on the ball's spatial location, but with a single global vector, all positional information is lost.
Era 2: Show, Attend and Tell — Spatial Attention

Xu et al. (2015) proposed retaining the CNN's spatial feature map rather than reducing it to a single vector. A 14×14 grid of 2048-dimensional vectors (one per spatial location) is passed to the decoder. At each time step, an attention mechanism computes a weighted average over all grid locations, producing a context vector that emphasises the region most relevant to the current word.
uses a normalised softmax over the spatial positions (differentiable end-to-end), while
samples one location stochastically (requires REINFORCE). Soft attention became the dominant choice due to easier training.

class SoftAttention(nn.Module):
    """Bahdanau-style soft attention over spatial CNN features."""
    def __init__(self, encoder_dim=2048, decoder_dim=512, attn_dim=256):
        super().__init__()
        self.encoder_attn = nn.Linear(encoder_dim, attn_dim)
        self.decoder_attn = nn.Linear(decoder_dim, attn_dim)
        self.full_attn = nn.Linear(attn_dim, 1)
        self.relu = nn.ReLU()
        self.softmax = nn.Softmax(dim=1)

    def forward(self, encoder_out, decoder_hidden):
        # encoder_out: (B, num_pixels, encoder_dim) — spatial feature map
        # decoder_hidden: (B, decoder_dim) — LSTM hidden state
        attn1 = self.encoder_attn(encoder_out)              # (B, num_pixels, attn_dim)
        attn2 = self.decoder_attn(decoder_hidden).unsqueeze(1)  # (B, 1, attn_dim)
        attn = self.full_attn(self.relu(attn1 + attn2)).squeeze(2)  # (B, num_pixels)
        alpha = self.softmax(attn)                          # attention weights
        # Weighted sum of spatial features
        context = (encoder_out * alpha.unsqueeze(2)).sum(dim=1)   # (B, encoder_dim)
        return context, alpha

at each decoding step can be visualised as a heat map over the image, yielding interpretable "where the model looked" for each generated word. This became one of the most-reproduced visualisations in deep learning.

Replacing both the CNN and the LSTM with transformer components unlocked scaling. Modern models tokenise images into patch embeddings (ViT) and decode captions with transformer decoders that use cross-attention to attend to visual tokens at every layer — not just at the input step, as in RNN-attention hybrids.

The key architectural change is that every decoder layer attends to image patch tokens, so the model can refine its visual context continuously as language context accumulates — rather than computing attention once at the start of each LSTM step.

BLIP (Bootstrapping Language-Image Pre-training, Li et al. 2022) addressed a major practical problem: most pre-training data for vision-language models comes from noisy web-scraped image-text pairs, where the alt-text is only loosely related to the image content.

BLIP uses a captioner trained on clean data to generate synthetic captions for web images, and a filter trained to discard captions that don't match their images. This bootstrapping cycle iteratively improves both caption quality and model capability. The architecture itself — called the Multimodal Encoder-Decoder (MED) — can operate in three modes: image-text contrastive learning, image-text matching, and image-conditioned language generation. All three modes share weights, enabling flexible multi-task learning.

from transformers import BlipProcessor, BlipForConditionalGeneration
from PIL import Image
import torch

processor = BlipProcessor.from_pretrained("Salesforce/blip-image-captioning-large")
model = BlipForConditionalGeneration.from_pretrained(
    "Salesforce/blip-image-captioning-large"
).eval()

image = Image.open("dog_at_park.jpg").convert("RGB")

# Unconditional captioning
inputs = processor(images=image, return_tensors="pt")
with torch.no_grad():
    output_ids = model.generate(
        **inputs,
        max_new_tokens=50,
        num_beams=5,
        length_penalty=1.0
    )
caption = processor.decode(output_ids[0], skip_special_tokens=True)
print(caption)  # "a golden retriever playing fetch in a park"

# Conditional captioning — guide with a prefix
inputs_cond = processor(
    images=image,
    text="The image shows",
    return_tensors="pt"
)
with torch.no_grad():
    output_cond = model.generate(**inputs_cond, max_new_tokens=40)
print(processor.decode(output_cond[0], skip_special_tokens=True))
# "The image shows a dog running on a green lawn"
BLIP-2 (Li et al. 2023) introduced a lightweight

(Querying Transformer) to bridge a frozen image encoder and a frozen large language model. Training a full vision-language model from scratch is prohibitively expensive; BLIP-2 instead keeps both the ViT image encoder and a pretrained LLM (OPT or Vicuna/LLaMA) completely frozen and trains only the Q-Former, which has around 188M parameters.

The Q-Former maintains 32 learnable query tokens that interact with the frozen image encoder via cross-attention, then passes its output to the LLM as a compact visual representation. This means the LLM never has to process raw patch tokens — it sees only 32 condensed visual tokens regardless of image resolution.

from transformers import Blip2Processor, Blip2ForConditionalGeneration
import torch
from PIL import Image

device = "cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu"

# BLIP-2 with OPT-2.7B backend
processor = Blip2Processor.from_pretrained("Salesforce/blip2-opt-2.7b")
model = Blip2ForConditionalGeneration.from_pretrained(
    "Salesforce/blip2-opt-2.7b",
    torch_dtype=torch.float16   # save memory
).to(device).eval()

image = Image.open("street_scene.jpg")

# Visual question answering with captioning model
prompt = "Question: What is the weather like? Answer:"
inputs = processor(images=image, text=prompt, return_tensors="pt").to(device, torch.float16)

with torch.no_grad():
    output = model.generate(**inputs, max_new_tokens=30)

print(processor.decode(output[0], skip_special_tokens=True).strip())
# "The weather appears sunny with clear skies."

(Lin et al., 2014), which contains 123,000 images each annotated with five human-written captions. The Karpathy split (113,000 train / 5,000 val / 5,000 test) is used in almost every published comparison.

Machine-generated captions are evaluated by comparing them against a set of human reference captions using automatic metrics. All metrics have blind spots; CIDEr correlates best with human judgements for captioning specifically.
Unigram F-score with synonym matching and stemming
TF-IDF-weighted n-gram similarity; rewards consensus with all references
Sensitive to reference set quality; computed per-corpus
Semantic propositional image caption evaluation via scene graphs
CLIP embedding cosine similarity between image and generated caption

CIDEr's TF-IDF weighting rewards captions that mention salient, image-specific content ("surfboard", "golden retriever") rather than generic words ("a", "the", "person"). This property makes it the primary optimisation target in reinforcement-learning-based caption training.

Standard cross-entropy training of captioning models maximises the log-likelihood of ground-truth tokens — but the evaluation metric (CIDEr) is not differentiable. Self-Critical Sequence Training (Rennie et al., 2017) bridges this gap by using REINFORCE: the model samples a caption and receives the CIDEr score as a reward, using the greedy-decoded caption as the baseline.

SCST reward signal: r(caption) = CIDEr(sampled) − CIDEr(greedy baseline). Positive reward means the sample was better than greedy; the model is nudged to produce it more often. Negative reward means greedy was better; the sample is suppressed.

SCST-based fine-tuning typically adds 5–10 CIDEr points on top of a cross-entropy-pretrained model, and is a standard step in all competitive captioning pipelines. The key implementation detail is that the baseline must be computed without gradient tracking — so that only the policy gradient flows through the sampled caption.

Standard image captioning produces one sentence describing the whole image. Dense captioning (Johnson et al., 2016) detects multiple regions of interest and generates a separate description for each. This requires simultaneously localising regions (bounding boxes) and generating their captions.
Modern vision-language models extend this to
(find the region described by text) and
(caption a specific region). Models like VisualBERT, VinVL, and MDETR unify these tasks.

Captioning models trained on MS-COCO describe everyday scenes well but may produce generic or inaccurate captions for specialised domains (medical imaging, satellite imagery, product photography). Fine-tuning on domain-specific paired data is the standard adaptation approach.

from transformers import BlipProcessor, BlipForConditionalGeneration
from torch.utils.data import Dataset, DataLoader
from PIL import Image
import torch

class CaptionDataset(Dataset):
    def __init__(self, image_paths, captions, processor):
        self.image_paths = image_paths
        self.captions = captions
        self.processor = processor

    def __len__(self):
        return len(self.image_paths)

    def __getitem__(self, idx):
        image = Image.open(self.image_paths[idx]).convert("RGB")
        caption = self.captions[idx]
        encoding = self.processor(
            images=image,
            text=caption,
            return_tensors="pt",
            padding="max_length",
            max_length=64,
            truncation=True
        )
        # Remove batch dim added by processor
        return {k: v.squeeze(0) for k, v in encoding.items()}

processor = BlipProcessor.from_pretrained("Salesforce/blip-image-captioning-base")
model = BlipForConditionalGeneration.from_pretrained(
    "Salesforce/blip-image-captioning-base"
)

# Domain-specific training data
dataset = CaptionDataset(image_paths, captions, processor)
loader = DataLoader(dataset, batch_size=16, shuffle=True)

optimizer = torch.optim.AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=5e-6, weight_decay=0.01)

for epoch in range(5):
    model.train()
    total_loss = 0
    for batch in loader:
        input_ids = batch["input_ids"]
        pixel_values = batch["pixel_values"]
        attention_mask = batch["attention_mask"]

        # Labels: shift right (BLIP uses LM loss internally)
        labels = input_ids.clone()
        labels[labels == processor.tokenizer.pad_token_id] = -100  # ignore padding

        outputs = model(
            pixel_values=pixel_values,
            input_ids=input_ids,
            attention_mask=attention_mask,
            labels=labels
        )
        loss = outputs.loss
        optimizer.zero_grad()
        loss.backward()
        torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), 1.0)
        optimizer.step()
        total_loss += loss.item()
    print(f"Epoch {epoch}: loss = {total_loss / len(loader):.4f}")
One model, many vision tasks via task tokens

(MLLMs) where captioning is one capability among many: the same model can answer questions, follow instructions, perform OCR, and describe images — all through a unified language modelling objective. Captioning benchmarks now serve mainly as regression tests for visual grounding within these larger systems.

: each generation addressed the key failure of the previous — global pooling loses spatial info; attention over a fixed CNN grid loses contextual language reasoning; transformers unify both with cross-attention at every decoder layer.
: its TF-IDF weighting rewards image-specific salient content over generic language, making it the best-correlated automatic metric with human preference for captioning.
: 32 learnable query tokens bridge a frozen ViT and a frozen LLM with only ~188M trainable parameters, making high-quality captioning achievable without training the full model stack.
: reinforcement learning with the greedy output as the baseline aligns the model directly with non-differentiable metrics like CIDEr, typically adding 5–10 points over cross-entropy training alone.

: COCO-trained models describe everyday scenes well but fail on medical, satellite, or product imagery. Fine-tuning on domain-specific (image, caption) pairs with a learning rate of ~5e-6 is the standard recipe.

: standalone captioning models are being replaced by general multimodal LLMs (LLaVA, InstructBLIP, GPT-4V) that handle captioning as one instruction among many, eliminating the need for task-specific model architectures.
